posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
• 2014 Caroline Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Caillerets - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru (12/27/2016)
    Rocking! This has a beautiful feminine touch. Bright fruit. Great acid/fruit balance. Perfect volume to me. Firm mineral presence. Love it.

Posted from CellarTracker

Watching a few of the bowls today after a rough night of call. This is such a feminine beautiful wine. A+ Great job Pierre-Yves!

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: Why great job Pierre-Yves? I thought this wine was done entirely by Caroline?

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Another winning 2014 white Burg from the Morey clan—glad it showed so well, Don.

A very happy and healthy 2017 to you, Pam, Trey, Ashley and the rest of the family.

Mike

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note Don. Bought some of her wines but have not tasted any yet.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Pierre Yves does all the vinification of her whites if my information is correct.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Thank you for the good notes, Don. I have little doubt that the wine is lovely. If I understand the situation correctly, the domaine of Caroline’s father, Jean-Marc Morey, was allocated between Caroline and her brother Sylvain following the 2013 vintage, and that this wine comes from Caroline’s parcel of Caillerets. I tried Sylvain’s 2014 Chassagne-Montrachet Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge, and it is a lovely wine, showing a touch similar to that of his father (which I did not find in his 2014 Santenay Grand Clos Rousseau). I did not purchase it, however, as he has moved away from his father’s standard size bottle and adopted that of PYCM, as Caroline has done, I believe. I know that you have some close contact with the family and I wonder if you have any insight as to why they use the large, heavy bottles and whether there might be an inoffensive way to encourage them to change.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Marty,
I really don’t have any insight into the bottle size. It is a very awkward size and doesn’t fit in any standard racking. I will ask my friends who have closer contact than I do.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Please report back, Don. The family certainly does not need the bottles to make a statement as the wines do that just fine.

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked: Re: bottle size/shape: I did ask the same question to the Belgian importer of PYCM a year ago and it is apparently about quality of preservation and minimisation of premox risk. Can’t remember the explanation as to why this bottle shape has that effect.

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: I also very much liked the Caillerets from Caroline. I tasted both the PYCM’s and Caroline’s Caillerets earlier this year and they are from different plots in the vineyard but similar wines. I was just at the new PYCM facility - it is beautiful and very functional since he designed the features. He was bottling some St. Aubin when I was there in an amazing bottling machine that is a long way from the old days of hand-filling and licking the labels.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Jerry,
Sounds like a great visit.
I haven’t had the pleasure of seeing his new facility.
It sounds beautiful!

posts.cooked:

Fascinating. How similar did the two wines taste. Did they taste like they were made by the same producer or two different producers.

posts.name: Dale Bowers
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note. Don’t have any Les Caillerets but do have some Les Chaumees and Champs Gain. Hope they are as good as your note.

dcornutt:

Pierre Yves does all the vinification of her whites if my information is correct.

posts.name: Dale Bowers

Its absolutely worth it
So this wine is a bargain?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:

Does he farm PYCM and she farm wines from her estates or does she do the farming for both and he do the winemaking for both? If the former, are there major differences in how they farm?

This I can’t answer but I think she is primarily responsible for her own properties re: vineyard work. This is my information. Subject to change with a better source. As far as whether she helps Pierre-Yves with his properties I have no idea. I know he vinifies the whites for both domaines according to my source. (At least he did in 2014)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Claus Jeppesen:

The 2014 PYCM Cailletets is 33% more expensive in eu
Its absolutely worth it
So this wine is a bargain?

Not sure that follows. The Caillerets from PY is a domaine wine and he has farmed it for a long time now. Have no idea if Caroline’s parcel is similarly well farmed, or if the vine age or genetics are as good. There are more determinants of quality in Burgundy than appellation and winemaker.

According to other posters. I have bought the wines, and in 2015 at least they seemed a bit fatter in style, but I am never offered them to taste and have always forgotten to ask.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Caroline shares cellar space with Pierre-Yves but does all of the vinification of her wines separately.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Caroline shares cellar space with Pierre-Yves but does all of the vinification of her wines separately.

Jeremy,

Thanks. Do you know whether they do things similarly or whether there are significant differences in winemaking or viticultural practices where we can expect major differences in the quality or style of the wines?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: I can’t answer that, but from the Caillerets wines I’ve tasted from both (only two from Caroline, quite a few from Pierre-Yves), I’d say the wines share more in common than they differ. Both are in the top tier of each portfolio, IMO.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Howard, both make lovely wines. The Caroline Morey wines I have tried don’t seem to have quite as much reduction and are perhaps ever so slightly more fruit forward.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Thanks.

I’ve visited Caroline for the last two vintages - 2015 and 2016 - a few notes:

  1. The vines of Domaine Jean-Marc Morey are now shared between Caroline and her brother, Jean-Marc having stopped working after the 2016 vintage (Nov 2016 had 2.5 ha but lost 80% of her Chassagne)
  2. The 2015s were rounder and less reductive than those of PY - but - those of PY also much less so than in recent years. (Nov 2016)
  3. In 2017 Caroline’s domaine increased to 7 hectares of vines, 35% of the domaine is now red, (Nov 2017)
  4. I’ve used more standard size barrels in this vintage vs demi-muids, simply because there is less wine. (Nov 2017 - unlike PY)
  5. Caroline’s Ch.Caillerets from the 3 different parcels covering all the levels of the vines, different slopes, three different plantations, but the average is 45-50 year-old vines - effectively a continuous line of vines from the road to the forest vs the two separate parcels of PY

My summary of her 2016s last November: Really a style difference vs Pierre-Yves – impressively-so when you think that they are all made in the same cuverie. Do you want to be challenged (PY) or do you want the wine to take over (Caroline)? Nervous (PY) or calm (Caroline)? The red wines here in 2016 are not the easy, juicy, ripe fruit of some domaines, they are a little more cerebral and pure.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
BTW, as much as I love the Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey Chassagne Caillerets, this wine from Caroline is my choice of the two now. I love the feminine character. Both retain that firm mineral feel of the vineyard. Her wines are more relaxed but retain the intensity and tension of the vineyard site. I like that. The wines are beautiful. The 2015 version is fantastic!
She is a genius with red wines. I am saving quite a few Santenays to see what they become. Her version of the Santenay Grand Clos Rousseau from her father’s time at Jean-Marc Morey is a thing of beauty. I wished her brother Sylvain had her touch.

Thanks for all the first hand information above from Jeremy and Bill. William’s point of view about the vineyard plots being very different is important to me.

When will she start to produce the Grand Clos Rousseau?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Maybe never. Sylvain has this at least right now.

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: Having the '14 Caillerets from Caroline tonight. Beautifully young, lots of body with plenty of acidity, vanilla, almond notes and amazing length. Lots of life in this wine.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
Having the '14 Caillerets from Caroline tonight. Beautifully young, lots of body with plenty of acidity, vanilla, almond notes and amazing length. Lots of life in this wine.

I’m liking ‘14 whites a lot.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Having the '14 Caillerets from Caroline tonight. Beautifully young, lots of body with plenty of acidity, vanilla, almond notes and amazing length. Lots of life in this wine.

I’m liking ‘14 whites a lot.

For me, this is the best vintage for white Burgundy I have ever tasted.
